headline: Four Undangs Purported to Depose the Ruler. State Government Says One Was No Longer in Office.
context: On 19 April 2026, four Undangs of Negeri Sembilan signed a declaration purporting to depose Tuanku Muhriz and naming Tunku Nadzaruddin as proposed 12th Ruler. The state government rejected it, saying one of the four had been dismissed effective 13 May 2025.

cards.big: Four Undangs signed a declaration purporting to depose Tuanku Muhriz. The state government rejected it, citing the dismissal of one signatory.
cards.sub: The same Undang who proclaimed Muhriz in 2008 signed this declaration. The MB said that Undang had been dismissed effective 13 May 2025.

cards.big: The state constitution lets the Undangs collectively call the Ruler to withdraw. Whether three alone (without the dismissed fourth) suffice is untested.
cards.sub: Rejection rests primarily on Mubarak's status; legal analysis also questioned whether a full and complete enquiry occurred.

cards.big: The same Undang who read the deposal on 19 April 2026 had proclaimed Tuanku Muhriz as 11th Ruler on 29 December 2008.
cards.sub: In 1967, the Undang bypassed Muhriz for the throne at 19, electing his uncle Tuanku Ja'afar. Tunku Nadzaruddin (Ja'afar's son) is now the proposed successor.

cards.big: The MB said Mubarak was dismissed effective 13 May 2025 over 33 alleged offences against Telaga Undang and Luak Sungei Ujong customs.
cards.sub: BERNAMA: the DKU sitting on 17 April 2026 accepted the Telaga Undang consensus dismissing Mubarak. Ibu Soko Klana Hulu declined to give him a mandate.

cards.big: Telaga Undang Sarifah Norzaidah called Mubarak's action treasonous (menderhaka). Police reports were made.
cards.sub: No public adjudication of the underlying misconduct claim was located. Public responses focused on the declaration's validity, not the allegation.

cards.big: The real question is not whether one disputed Undang can act. Two questions matter: was the deposal enquiry full and complete, and can three Undangs invoke the provision alone?
Some institutions rejected the declaration and defended Tuanku Muhriz's position. No court has ruled. The constitutional and customary questions remain open.
